era_context: Late 14th century: The Ottoman Empire under Murad I is at the height of its Balkan expansion. The Hundred Years' War continues in Western Europe with shifting military fortunes. The Ming Dynasty is firmly established in China under the Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398), who is consolidating Han Chinese rule after the Yuan. The Goryeo Dynasty in Korea is in its final years before the Joseon takeover in 1392. This is a period of dynastic consolidation and military competition across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (pre-Columbian sources are sparse)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary written records)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's purges and administrative consolidation
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his systematic consolidation of power through purges of officials suspected of disloyalty or corruption. These purges, which had begun in the 1370s and would continue into the 1390s, were part of a broader effort to centralize authority and eliminate potential rivals. The emperor was establishing a highly personalized autocratic system, reducing the power of the imperial bureaucracy and increasing imperial prerogative. These actions had profound effects on Ming governance and set precedents for later emperors.
key_figures: Various officials purged during this period

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's purges are well-documented in official records, though specific events in 1388 are not individually highlighted. The year falls within the broader pattern of consolidation that characterized his entire reign.

events.title: Battle of Aljubarrota aftermath and Portuguese consolidation
events.description: Following the Portuguese victory at Aljubarrota in 1385, King John I of Portugal was consolidating his position and establishing the Aviz Dynasty. By 1388, the king had secured his throne against Castilian claims and was beginning to establish Portugal as an independent maritime power. The Treaty of Windsor (1386) with England had formalized an alliance that would shape Portuguese foreign policy for centuries. This period saw the beginning of Portuguese expansion and the development of naval capabilities that would lead to the Age of Exploration.
key_figures: Philippa of Lancaster (John's English wife, married 1387)

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Fernão Lopes, Crónica de El-Rei D. João I)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of John I's rule is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The year 1388 does not mark a specific major event, but rather represents the ongoing stabilization of the Portuguese state following the 1385 victory.

events.title: Goryeo Dynasty's final years and internal instability
events.description: The Goryeo Dynasty was in its final years of rule, facing internal instability and military pressure from the rising Yi Seong-gye faction. By 1388, the state was weakened by factional conflicts, Mongol legacy institutions, and the rise of military strongmen. Yi Seong-gye's refusal to invade Manchuria (the Wihwado Retreat) in 1388 was a pivotal moment that demonstrated the power of military commanders and foreshadowed the dynasty's imminent collapse. The Goryeo court was losing control of its military and administrative apparatus.
key_figures: Yi Seong-gye (military commander, future founder of Joseon)

sources.name: Goryeosa (History of Goryeo), compiled in Joseon period
Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general instability of late Goryeo is well-documented, and Yi Seong-gye's rise is confirmed by multiple sources. The Wihwado Retreat is a key event, though its exact date and significance are interpreted differently by historians.

events.title: Wihwado Retreat (Yi Seong-gye's refusal to invade Manchuria)
events.description: Yi Seong-gye, commanding Goryeo forces, was ordered to invade Manchuria to recover territory lost to the Ming Dynasty. Instead, he led his army in a retreat back to the capital, effectively refusing the order. This act of insubordination demonstrated the weakness of the Goryeo court and the power of military commanders. The retreat is traditionally viewed as a pivotal moment that led directly to Yi Seong-gye's seizure of power and the founding of the Joseon Dynasty four years later. However, modern historians debate whether this was a calculated political move or a pragmatic military decision.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Wihwado Retreat is confirmed by multiple Korean sources, though the exact date (some sources place it in 1388, others in 1388 or 1389) and the motivations behind it are debated. The event's significance as a turning point is accepted by modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The Wihwado Retreat's significance has been reassessed by modern historians. While traditionally portrayed as a deliberate political move by Yi Seong-gye to seize power, some scholars argue it was a pragmatic military decision based on the impossibility of the campaign. The retreat did not immediately lead to Yi's seizure of power—four years elapsed before the Joseon Dynasty was founded, suggesting the political transition was more complex than a simple coup. Additionally, the Hongwu Emperor's purges in Ming China, while documented in official records, may have been exaggerated in later compilations to justify the emperor's autocratic rule.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1388 is moderate to good for East Asia and Western Europe, but sparse for other regions. Korean sources are abundant but were compiled after the Joseon Dynasty's founding, introducing potential bias toward narratives that justified the new regime. Chinese official records are systematic but reflect the Hongwu Emperor's perspective and priorities. Portuguese sources are well-preserved but focus on elite political and military history. Ottoman and Balkan sources for this year are limited to later chronicles. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia are largely absent from surviving written records for this period.

graph_edges.note: The Wihwado Retreat demonstrated the weakness of Goryeo and the power of Yi Seong-gye, leading to his seizure of power and founding of Joseon Dynasty
Hongwu's consolidation efforts continued throughout his reign, shaping Ming governance
Portuguese consolidation under John I set the stage for the Age of Exploration under his son Henry the Navigator
